Visitor.title: Visitor
Visitor.description: Represents an individual visitor to the library, storing their identifying information.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Visitor entity.

institutionalId.description: The official ID provided by the institution (e.g., student ID, employee ID) for visitor identification.

email.description: The institutional email address of the visitor, used for identification.

fullName.description: The full name of the visitor.

collegeOrOffice.description: The college or office the visitor is affiliated with within the institution.

Purpose.title: Purpose
Purpose.description: Represents a predefined common purpose for visiting the library, often suggested by an AI tool.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Purpose entity.

name.description: The descriptive name of the purpose (e.g., 'Study', 'Research', 'Meeting').

description.description: A more detailed explanation of the purpose.

VisitLog.title: VisitLog
VisitLog.description: Records each instance of a visitor signing into and out of the library.

id.description: Unique identifier for the VisitLog entry.

visitorId.description: Reference to the Visitor entity who made this visit. (Relationship: Visitor 1:N VisitLog)

timeIn.description: Timestamp indicating when the visitor signed into the library.

timeOut.description: Timestamp indicating when the visitor signed out of the library. Null or missing for active sessions.

purposeId.description: Reference to the Purpose entity describing the primary reason for the visit. (Relationship: Purpose 1:N VisitLog)

customPurposeDescription.description: Optional free-text field for a purpose not covered by predefined categories.

BlockEntry.title: BlockEntry
BlockEntry.description: Manages records of visitors who are blocked from accessing the library, along with reasons and status.

id.description: Unique identifier for the BlockEntry entity.

visitorId.description: Reference to the Visitor entity who is blocked. (Relationship: Visitor 1:N BlockEntry)

blockReason.description: A detailed explanation of why the visitor has been blocked.

blockedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the block was initiated.

isActive.description: Boolean flag indicating if the block is currently active (true) or has been lifted (false).

definition.description: Collection storing UIDs of users who have administrative privileges. The mere existence of a document signifies admin status (DBAC).

params.name: adminId
params.description: The Firebase Authentication UID of an administrator.

definition.description: Collection for predefined visit purposes. Globally readable for visitor sign-in, writeable only by administrators.

params.name: purposeId
params.description: Unique identifier for the Purpose entity.

definition.description: Collection storing profiles of library visitors. Access is restricted to administrators and trusted backend services for read/write operations.

params.name: visitorId
params.description: Unique identifier for the Visitor entity.

definition.description: Collection recording each library visit. Access is restricted to administrators. Includes denormalized 'visitorFullName' and 'visitorCollegeOrOffice' for authorization independence and QAP support.

params.name: visitLogId
params.description: Unique identifier for the VisitLog entry.

definition.description: Collection managing records of blocked visitors. Access is restricted to administrators. Includes denormalized 'visitorFullName' and 'visitorInstitutionalId' for authorization independence and QAP support.

params.name: blockEntryId
params.description: Unique identifier for the BlockEntry entity.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure prioritizes security, scalability, and debuggability by strictly adhering to the core design principles and strategy mandates. Authorization Independence is paramount, achieved primarily through strategic denormalization and structural segregation, ensuring robust and simple security rules.

**Authorization Independence:**
1.  **Admin Role Segregation (`/roles_admin/{adminId}`):** Administrative privileges are granted by the mere existence of a document in the `/roles_admin` collection, where `adminId` is the `request.auth.uid`. This allows for a simple, atomic existence check in security rules, completely avoiding hierarchical `get()` calls to determine user roles or permissions. This fulfills DBAC (Database-Backed Access Control) by storing roles in the database.
2.  **Centralized Institutional Data Ownership:** Core application data entities such as `Visitor` profiles, `VisitLog` entries, and `BlockEntry` records are treated as institution-owned data, rather than individually user-owned. This design choice means that access to these collections is primarily restricted to authenticated administrators. The security rules for `/visitors`, `/visitLogs`, and `/blockEntries` will check for admin status via `/roles_admin`, eliminating any need for `get()` calls to check for ownership or related document attributes for authorization within the rules.
3.  **Denormalization for Context:** To enhance data clarity and support QAPs without `get()` calls in rules, critical contextual data is denormalized:
    *   Each `VisitLog` document (`/visitLogs/{visitLogId}`) will include denormalized `visitorFullName` and `visitorCollegeOrOffice` fields. This ensures that when an administrator retrieves a `VisitLog`, all necessary display information about the visitor is immediately available within that single document, removing the need for additional client-side reads or complex rules to fetch visitor details.
    *   Similarly, each `BlockEntry` document (`/blockEntries/{blockEntryId}`) will contain denormalized `visitorFullName` and `visitorInstitutionalId`. This makes each block entry self-contained for administrative review and reporting.
4.  **Backend for Complex Operations:** The visitor sign-in process, which involves looking up or creating `Visitor` profiles, checking `BlockEntry` status, and creating `VisitLog` entries, will be mediated by a trusted backend service (e.g., Cloud Functions). This backend service, operating with administrative privileges, will handle the multi-document reads and writes required for a secure and atomic sign-in flow. This strategy offloads complex authorization logic from client-side Firestore rules, thus preventing rule-based `get()` dependencies and ensuring that the rules remain declarative and performant.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):**
1.  **Structural Segregation:** The principle of Structural Segregation ensures that all documents within a given collection share a homogeneous security posture, which is key for QAPs.
    *   The `/purposes` collection is designed for global read access (e.g., `allow read: if request.auth.uid != null;` if the sign-in portal requires anonymous auth, or simply `if true;` for fully public access). This allows any visitor (or the sign-in portal) to list all available purposes without encountering documents they are not authorized to view, as all documents are equally accessible.
    *   The `/visitors`, `/visitLogs`, and `/blockEntries` collections are designated as admin-only. An administrator, whose status is verified by the existence of their UID in `/roles_admin`, has full read access to *all* documents within these collections. Therefore, any `list` operation performed by an admin on these collections will return a complete and fully authorized dataset, fulfilling the QAP requirement that all returned documents satisfy the security conditions.

This structure ensures clear authorization intent, simplifies security rules by isolating access patterns, and optimizes for secure, scalable `list` operations.
